title: SigmaSeparationSymmetric
tex_block_html: Note that $\sigma$-separation is symmetric: $A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C$, since when $J = \emptyset$ the set of walks between $A$ and $B$ is the same regardless of direction, and the $\sigma$-blocking conditions are invariant under walk reversal.

\begin{Lem}[$\sigma$-separation is symmetric]\label{lem:sigma_separation_symmetric}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}, the walk definition of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i., the $\sigma$-blocking predicate (``$\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked'') of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}, and the $i\sigma$-/$\sigma$-separation predicates of def \ref{def:sigma_separation}. Suppose
    \[
        J = \emptyset,
    \]
    so that, by def \ref{def:sigma_separation}, item~4, the $\sigma$-separation abbreviation
    \[
        A \sPerp_G B \given C \;\; := \;\; A \isPerp_G B \given C
    \]
    is in scope (and, as recorded there, $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ unfolds under $J = \emptyset$ to: for every integer $n \ge 0$ and every walk $\pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i., with $v_0 \in A$ and $v_n \in B$, the walk $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked in the sense of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}).
    Let $A, B, C \ins V$ be subsets of nodes --- equivalently $A, B, C \ins J \cup V$, since $J = \emptyset$; $A$, $B$, $C$ are \emph{not} assumed to be pairwise disjoint, nor non-empty.

    Then $\sigma$-separation in $G$ is symmetric in its first two arguments:
    \[
        A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C.
    \]
\end{Lem}

\end{document}

\begin{Lem}[$\sigma$-separation is symmetric]\label{lem:sigma_separation_symmetric}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}, the walk definition of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i., the $\sigma$-blocking predicate (``$\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked'') of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}, and the $i\sigma$-/$\sigma$-separation predicates of def \ref{def:sigma_separation}. Suppose
    \[
        J = \emptyset,
    \]
    so that, by def \ref{def:sigma_separation}, item~4, the $\sigma$-separation abbreviation
    \[
        A \sPerp_G B \given C \;\; := \;\; A \isPerp_G B \given C
    \]
    is in scope (and, as recorded there, $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ unfolds under $J = \emptyset$ to: for every integer $n \ge 0$ and every walk $\pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i., with $v_0 \in A$ and $v_n \in B$, the walk $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked in the sense of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}).
    Let $A, B, C \ins V$ be subsets of nodes --- equivalently $A, B, C \ins J \cup V$, since $J = \emptyset$; $A$, $B$, $C$ are \emph{not} assumed to be pairwise disjoint, nor non-empty.

    Then $\sigma$-separation in $G$ is symmetric in its first two arguments:
    \[
        A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C.
    \]
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
The proof has three main beats:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(I)] unfold $\sPerp_G$ via def \ref{def:sigma_separation}, item~4, into the universal-over-walks statement of def \ref{def:sigma_separation}, item~1, and reduce the biconditional to its forward implication (the converse follows by swapping $A$ and $B$ in the same argument);
    \item[(II)] construct an explicit walk-reversal involution $\pi \mapsto \pi^*$ that is a bijection between walks $v_0 \to v_n$ and walks $v_n \to v_0$ in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.; and
    \item[(III)] show that the $C$-$\sigma$-blocking predicate of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking} is invariant under that reversal, so that the universal-over-walks statement is symmetric in $A$ and $B$.
\end{enumerate}
The substantive content lives in beats (II) and (III): once walk reversal is set up as an involution that maps $C$-$\sigma$-blocked walks to $C$-$\sigma$-blocked walks (and back), the biconditional drops out by direct chase.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{(I) Unfold and reduce to one direction.}
Throughout, fix $G = (J, V, E, L)$ a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}) with $J = \emptyset$, and $A, B, C \ins V = J \cup V$. By def \ref{def:sigma_separation}, item~4, the abbreviation $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ is in scope and unfolds (under $J = \emptyset$, so $J \cup B = B$ and $J \cup A = A$) to
\[
    A \sPerp_G B \given C
    \quad\iff\quad
    \forall\, n \ge 0, \;\forall\, \text{walks } \pi = (v_0, a_0, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n) \text{ in } G
    \text{ with } v_0 \in A,\, v_n \in B :\;
    \pi \text{ is } C\text{-}\sigma\text{-blocked},
\]
where ``walk in $G$'' is in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i., and ``$C$-$\sigma$-blocked'' is in the sense of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}. Symmetrically $B \sPerp_G A \given C$ unfolds to the analogous universal over walks $\pi'$ in $G$ with $v_0' \in B$ and $v_n' \in A$.

The biconditional we have to prove is symmetric in the roles of $A$ and $B$: the right-hand side $B \sPerp_G A \given C$ is obtained from the left-hand side $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ by literally swapping the symbols $A$ and $B$. It therefore suffices to prove the forward implication
\[
    A \sPerp_G B \given C \;\implies\; B \sPerp_G A \given C; \tag{$\star$}
\]
the converse implication then follows by applying $(\star)$ to the pair $(B, A)$ in place of $(A, B)$. From now on we assume $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ and aim to show $B \sPerp_G A \given C$.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{(II) Walk reversal.}
We construct, for every $n \ge 0$ and every walk $\pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ in $G$ (def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.), a \emph{reversed walk} $\pi^*$ from $v_n$ to $v_0$ in $G$. The construction is per slot, by the walk constraint of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.

For $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$, the walk constraint at slot $i$ of $\pi$ states
\[
    \bigl(a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \;\land\; a_i \in E \cup L\bigr)
    \;\lor\;
    \bigl(a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \;\land\; a_i \in E\bigr).
\]
The slot $i$ of $\pi$ contributes an edge $a_i \in E \cup L$ to the walk; we define the reversed edge $a_i^*$ at slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ as follows, by case-analysis on which disjunct of the walk constraint fires at slot $i$ of $\pi$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(F$_E$)] If $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (directed edge of $G$ with tail $v_i$ and head $v_{i+1}$, traversed forwards from $v_i$ to $v_{i+1}$ on $\pi$), set
        \[
            a_i^* := (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E,
        \]
        i.e.\ the \emph{same} directed graph-edge, but now occupying slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ between the nodes $v_{i+1}$ (left) and $v_i$ (right). The walk constraint of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ at slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ is then satisfied via its \emph{second} disjunct ($a_i^* = (\text{right-node}, \text{left-node}) = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$); a directed graph-edge that was traversed forwards on $\pi$ is read backwards on $\pi^*$.
    \item[(B$_E$)] If $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$ (directed graph-edge with tail $v_{i+1}$ and head $v_i$, traversed backwards from $v_i$ to $v_{i+1}$ on $\pi$), set
        \[
            a_i^* := (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E,
        \]
        i.e.\ the same directed graph-edge, now occupying slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ between $v_{i+1}$ (left) and $v_i$ (right). The walk constraint at slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ is satisfied via its \emph{first} disjunct ($a_i^* = (\text{left-node}, \text{right-node}) = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E \subseteq E \cup L$); a directed graph-edge traversed backwards on $\pi$ becomes one traversed forwards on $\pi^*$.
    \item[(L)] If $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$ (bidirected graph-edge of $G$), set
        \[
            a_i^* := (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in L.
        \]
        Here we use the standing convention of def \ref{def-cdmg} that the bidirected-edge set $L$ consists of unordered (symmetric) pairs of nodes: if $(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$ then $(v_{i+1}, v_i) \in L$. (Equivalently, $L$ is closed under the swap $(u, w) \leftrightarrow (w, u)$; in the LN-typed graph-edge set, an $L$-edge is unoriented.) The walk constraint at slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ is satisfied via its first disjunct ($a_i^* = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in L \subseteq E \cup L$).
\end{itemize}
The reversed walk is then the tuple
\[
    \pi^* \;:=\; (v_n, \; a_{n-1}^*, \; v_{n-1}, \; a_{n-2}^*, \; \dots, \; a_1^*, \; v_1, \; a_0^*, \; v_0),
\]
i.e.\ the same node sequence read in reverse, paired with the slot-by-slot edge construction above. By the per-slot verification, $\pi^*$ satisfies the walk constraint of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ at each of its $n$ slots, so $\pi^*$ is a walk in $G$ from $v_n$ to $v_0$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Involution.}
Each of the three cases above is its own inverse: applying the construction once produces an edge whose ``forward'' vs.\ ``backward'' traversal is flipped, and applying it a second time flips it back. Concretely, in case (F$_E$) the reverse of $\pi^*$ at slot $n - 1 - (n - 1 - i) = i$ is again $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (we read $\pi^*$'s slot $n - 1 - i$, whose stored edge is $(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$, in case (B$_E$) of the reversal construction applied to $\pi^*$ -- the second-disjunct walk-constraint case -- and recover the first-disjunct configuration of the original $\pi$). Cases (B$_E$) and (L) are analogous (the L case using $((v_{i+1}, v_i)$ swapped $) = (v_i, v_{i+1})$). Hence $(\pi^*)^* = \pi$ on the nose, so $\pi \mapsto \pi^*$ is an involution on the set of walks in $G$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Bijection between walk-spaces.}
For fixed source-set $A$ and target-set $B$, the involution restricts to a bijection between
\[
    \mathcal{W}_{A \to B} \;:=\; \{\,\pi \text{ walk in } G : v_0 \in A,\, v_n \in B\,\}
    \quad\text{and}\quad
    \mathcal{W}_{B \to A} \;:=\; \{\,\pi^* \text{ walk in } G : v_0^* \in B,\, v_n^* \in A\,\},
\]
since $\pi \in \mathcal{W}_{A \to B}$ has $v_0 \in A,\, v_n \in B$ and $\pi^*$ has its start-node equal to $\pi$'s end-node $v_n \in B$ and its end-node equal to $\pi$'s start-node $v_0 \in A$, so $\pi^* \in \mathcal{W}_{B \to A}$, and conversely by involution. (This resolves the wording-check item \texttt{walks\_same\_regardless\_of\_direction\_ambiguous} recorded in the workspace scratchpad for this row: walks are ordered sequences in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i., and the bijection between the two direction-sets is the explicit involution $\pi \leftrightarrow \pi^*$, not literal set equality.)

\medskip\noindent\textbf{(III) $\sigma$-blocking is invariant under reversal.}
We claim that, for every walk $\pi$ in $G$,
\[
    \pi \text{ is } C\text{-}\sigma\text{-blocked}
    \quad\iff\quad
    \pi^* \text{ is } C\text{-}\sigma\text{-blocked},
\tag{$\dagger$}
\]
both in the sense of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}. By the involution $(\pi^*)^* = \pi$, the two directions of $(\dagger)$ are mutually each other's converses, so it suffices to prove the forward direction. We do so by tracing each of the two existential disjuncts of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}, separately, through the position-shift $k \leftrightarrow n - k$ induced by reversal.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Position-shift bookkeeping.}
By construction (II), the node at position $k$ on $\pi$ -- namely $v_k$ -- is the node at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ (which reads $\pi$'s node sequence backwards). The walk-incident edges at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ are slot $n - k - 1$ of $\pi^*$ (when $n - k \ge 1$) and slot $n - k$ of $\pi^*$ (when $n - k \le n - 1$). By the slot indexing of (II), slot $j$ of $\pi^*$ corresponds to slot $n - 1 - j$ of $\pi$; so:
\begin{itemize}
    \item slot $n - k - 1$ of $\pi^*$ corresponds to slot $n - 1 - (n - k - 1) = k$ of $\pi$ (i.e.\ to $a_k$, the right-incident slot at position $k$ on $\pi$), with the edge stored as $a_k^*$ per case (F$_E$)/(B$_E$)/(L) above;
    \item slot $n - k$ of $\pi^*$ corresponds to slot $n - 1 - (n - k) = k - 1$ of $\pi$ (i.e.\ to $a_{k-1}$, the left-incident slot at position $k$ on $\pi$), with the edge stored as $a_{k-1}^*$.
\end{itemize}
In words: the left-incident slot of $\pi^*$ at position $n - k$ is the reversal of the right-incident slot of $\pi$ at position $k$, and the right-incident slot of $\pi^*$ at position $n - k$ is the reversal of the left-incident slot of $\pi$ at position $k$. The walk-incident index sets at position $k$ on $\pi$ and at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ are therefore in canonical bijection (with the two slots swapped).

\smallskip\noindent\emph{(III.a) Collider invariance.}
We show: position $k$ is a collider on $\pi$ (def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~ii.) iff position $n - k$ is a collider on $\pi^*$.

Per def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~ii., position $k$ is a collider on $\pi$ iff $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = 2$, i.e.\ both walk-incident edges at position $k$ -- $a_{k-1}$ (slot $k - 1$, present when $k \ge 1$) and $a_k$ (slot $k$, present when $k \le n - 1$) -- are ``edges into $v_k$'' in the sense of def \ref{def:collider_noncollider} (the formal predicate from def \ref{def-edge-relations}, item~ii.: ``$a_i \in E$ with right-component $v_k$, or $a_i \in L$ with either component $v_k$'').

We must show that, under the bijection of walk-incident slots described above, ``edge into $v_k$ at slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$'' translates to ``edge into $v_k$ at slot $n - k$ of $\pi^*$'' (the corresponding reversed-slot), and similarly slot $k$ of $\pi$ translates to slot $n - k - 1$ of $\pi^*$. We check each of the three case-clauses (F$_E$), (B$_E$), (L) of the reversal construction:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Case (F$_E$) at slot $i$ of $\pi$: $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$.}
        For $\pi$: $a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ iff its $E$-clause fires with second component $v_k$, iff $v_{i+1} = v_k$. (This happens at $i = k - 1$, giving an edge into $v_k$ at slot $k - 1$.)
        For $\pi^*$ at slot $n - 1 - i$: $a_i^* = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$, still a directed graph-edge, occupying slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ between the nodes $v_{i+1}$ (left) and $v_i$ (right). The right-component of $a_i^*$, in the sense of the $E$-clause of def \ref{def-edge-relations}, item~ii., is $v_{i+1}$ (the second component of the stored ordered pair). So $a_i^*$ is an edge into $v_k$ at $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ iff $v_{i+1} = v_k$, exactly as for $\pi$. Both walks register the same arrowhead at $v_k$.
    \item \emph{Case (B$_E$) at slot $i$ of $\pi$: $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$.}
        For $\pi$: $a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ iff $v_i = v_k$. (This happens at $i = k$, giving an edge into $v_k$ at slot $k$.)
        For $\pi^*$ at slot $n - 1 - i$: $a_i^* = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$, with right-component $v_i$. So $a_i^*$ is an edge into $v_k$ at $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ iff $v_i = v_k$, exactly as for $\pi$.
    \item \emph{Case (L) at slot $i$ of $\pi$: $a_i \in L$.}
        For $\pi$: $a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ iff its $L$-clause fires, i.e.\ $v_k$ is one of $a_i$'s two endpoints in $L$. By symmetry of $L$, this depends only on the underlying bidirected graph-edge -- a property invariant under the swap performed by case (L).
        For $\pi^*$: $a_i^* = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in L$ (same bidirected graph-edge as $a_i$, with the ordered-pair writing swapped). The $L$-clause then likewise fires iff $v_k$ is an endpoint -- the same condition.
\end{itemize}
In each case, ``$a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ in $G$ along $\pi$'' is equivalent to ``$a_i^*$ is an edge into $v_k$ in $G$ along $\pi^*$ at the corresponding reversed slot''.

Combining over the two walk-incident slots at position $k$ on $\pi$ (and at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$), we get
\[
    \mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \;=\; \mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k).
\]
In particular $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = 2$ iff $\mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k) = 2$, so position $k$ is a collider on $\pi$ iff position $n - k$ is a collider on $\pi^*$ (def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~ii.). The same equation $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = \mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k)$ gives the corresponding non-collider statement $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1 \iff \mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k) \le 1$ (def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.), which we use below.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{(III.b) Blockable non-collider invariance.}
We show: position $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ (def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}, paragraph ``Blockable non-collider on $\pi$'') iff position $n - k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi^*$.

Per def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}, position $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ iff (i)~it is a non-collider on $\pi$ (def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.), AND (ii)~the blockable disjunction holds at $k$ on $\pi$:
\[
    k \in \{0, n\}
    \;\;\lor\;\;
    \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k-1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr)
    \;\;\lor\;\;
    \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k+1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr).
\]
We show invariance of clauses (i) and (ii) separately.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Clause (i) -- non-collider status} -- is invariant under the position-shift $k \leftrightarrow n - k$ by (III.a): $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1 \iff \mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k) \le 1$.
    \item \emph{Clause (ii) -- the blockable disjunction} -- we check each of the three disjuncts.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \emph{End-position $k \in \{0, n\}$.} Under the position-shift $k \mapsto n - k$, the set $\{0, n\}$ is mapped to itself (with $0 \leftrightarrow n$), so $k \in \{0, n\}$ on $\pi$ iff $n - k \in \{0, n\}$ on $\pi^*$.
            \item \emph{Outgoing E-walk-edge at slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$ ($a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, with $v_{k-1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$).} This is configuration (B$_E$) of slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$ (with $v_{i+1} := v_k$ and $v_i := v_{k-1}$ in case (B$_E$)'s notation, the directed graph-edge being $(v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, tail $v_k$, head $v_{k-1}$, traversed backwards on $\pi$). Under reversal, case (B$_E$) sends slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$ to slot $n - 1 - (k - 1) = n - k$ of $\pi^*$, with stored edge $a_{k-1}^* = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$. The first disjunct of the walk constraint at slot $n - k$ of $\pi^*$ then fires: $a_{k-1}^* = (v_k, v_{k-1}) = (\text{left-node at slot } n - k \text{ of } \pi^*, \text{right-node at slot } n - k \text{ of } \pi^*)$ -- because the left node of slot $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ is the position-$n - k$ node $v_k$ and the right node is the position-$(n - k + 1)$ node $v_{k-1}$ -- and lives in $E$. Reading this against the predicate ``outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ at $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ on the right-incident slot $n - k$'' from def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}: the predicate ``$a_i \in E$ with first component $v_k$'' fires for $a_{k-1}^*$ (its first component is $v_k$, by construction), so $a_{k-1}^*$ is the outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ on the right-incident slot of $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$, with the other endpoint along $\pi^*$ being $v_{k-1}$ -- the same node as the other endpoint along $\pi$.

            Conversely, an outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ on the right-incident slot of $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ -- i.e.\ $a_{k-1}^* = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ -- reverses (by involution) to the same directed graph-edge at slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$. Specifically, applying construction (II) to $\pi^*$ at slot $n - k$ via case (F$_E$) gives back $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ at slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$, with tail $v_k$ at the position-$k$ end -- precisely the original outgoing E-walk-edge on $\pi$. Hence the ``outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on the left-incident slot of $\pi$'' corresponds bijectively to the ``outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $n - k$ on the right-incident slot of $\pi^*$''.
            \item \emph{Outgoing E-walk-edge at slot $k$ of $\pi$ ($a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$, with $v_{k+1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$).} This is configuration (F$_E$) of slot $k$ of $\pi$ (directed graph-edge $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$, tail $v_k$, head $v_{k+1}$, traversed forwards on $\pi$). Under reversal, case (F$_E$) sends slot $k$ of $\pi$ to slot $n - 1 - k$ of $\pi^*$, with stored edge $a_k^* = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. At slot $n - 1 - k$ of $\pi^*$, the left node is $v_{k+1}$ and the right node is $v_k$; the second disjunct of the walk constraint fires: $a_k^* = (\text{right-node}, \text{left-node}) = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. Reading this against the predicate ``outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ at $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ on the left-incident slot $n - k - 1$'' from def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}: the predicate ``$a_i \in E$ with first component $v_k$'' fires for $a_k^*$ (its first component is $v_k$, by construction), so $a_k^*$ is the outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ on the left-incident slot of $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$, with the other endpoint along $\pi^*$ being $v_{k+1}$ -- the same node as the other endpoint along $\pi$.
        \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
The disjunction at $k$ on $\pi$ -- end-position OR left-incident-slot outgoing E-walk-edge to a non-$\Sc^G(v_k)$ node OR right-incident-slot outgoing E-walk-edge to a non-$\Sc^G(v_k)$ node -- maps under the position-shift $k \leftrightarrow n - k$ to the disjunction at $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ -- end-position OR right-incident-slot outgoing E-walk-edge OR left-incident-slot outgoing E-walk-edge -- which is the \emph{same} three-disjunct, just with the two ``outgoing'' disjuncts in swapped order. The ``other endpoint $\notin \Sc^G(v_k)$'' clause attaches to the underlying graph-edge's other endpoint and to the node $v_k$ at the position, both of which transport verbatim under reversal; the membership predicate $\Sc^G(\cdot) = \Anc^G(\cdot) \cap \Desc^G(\cdot)$ (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~vii.) is a property of $G$ alone -- not of the walk -- and is therefore unaffected by reversal.

Combining: clause (i) and clause (ii) at $k$ on $\pi$ jointly transport, by (III.a) and the case-by-case check above, to clause (i) and clause (ii) at $n - k$ on $\pi^*$. Hence position $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ iff position $n - k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi^*$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{(III.c) $C$-$\sigma$-blocking invariance.}
We now show $(\dagger)$. Per def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}, $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked iff at least one of the following two existentials holds:
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(B1)] there exists a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ such that $k$ is a collider on $\pi$ and $v_k \notin \Anc^G(C)$;
    \item[(B2)] there exists a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ such that $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ and $v_k \in C$.
\end{itemize}
We must show the same disjunction holds for $\pi^*$.

Suppose (B1) holds for $\pi$, with witness position $k$ and node $v_k$. By (III.a), position $n - k$ is a collider on $\pi^*$, and the node at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ is the same node $v_k$ (the node identity transports under reversal: $\pi^*$'s node-sequence is just $\pi$'s node-sequence read backwards). The condition $v_k \notin \Anc^G(C)$ is a condition on the node $v_k$ and on $G$ alone (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~iv.; the ancestor set in $G$ is independent of any walk), so it is unaffected by which walk we are considering. Hence position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ is a collider with $v_k \notin \Anc^G(C)$, so (B1) holds for $\pi^*$ at position $n - k$.

Suppose (B2) holds for $\pi$, with witness position $k$ and node $v_k$. By (III.b), position $n - k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi^*$, and the node at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ is $v_k$. The condition $v_k \in C$ is a condition on the node $v_k$ alone, so it transports verbatim. Hence (B2) holds for $\pi^*$ at position $n - k$.

In either case, $\pi^*$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked. So $\pi$ $C$-$\sigma$-blocked $\Rightarrow$ $\pi^*$ $C$-$\sigma$-blocked; by the involution argument noted above, the converse follows, and $(\dagger)$ is established.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{(IV) The forward implication $(\star)$.}
Assume $A \sPerp_G B \given C$. By (I), this says: for every walk $\pi$ in $G$ with $v_0 \in A$ and $v_n \in B$, $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked.

Let $\pi' = (v_0', a_0', \dots, a_{n-1}', v_n')$ be an arbitrary walk in $G$ with $v_0' \in B$ and $v_n' \in A$. We must show $\pi'$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked. Apply the reversal construction of (II) to $\pi'$: by the bijection $\mathcal{W}_{B \to A} \to \mathcal{W}_{A \to B}$ (specialised at source-set $A$ and target-set $B$), $(\pi')^*$ is a walk in $G$ with start-node $v_n' \in A$ and end-node $v_0' \in B$, so $(\pi')^* \in \mathcal{W}_{A \to B}$. By the hypothesis $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ applied to $(\pi')^*$, the walk $(\pi')^*$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked. By (III) applied to $\pi'$ -- specifically, $(\dagger)$ applied to $\pi'$ gives $\pi'$ $C$-$\sigma$-blocked iff $(\pi')^*$ $C$-$\sigma$-blocked -- the walk $\pi'$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked. Since $\pi'$ was arbitrary, $B \sPerp_G A \given C$ holds, by the unfolding in (I).

\medskip\noindent\textbf{(V) Conclusion.}
By the reduction in (I), the biconditional $A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C$ follows from the forward implication $(\star)$, which is (IV); the converse implication is obtained by applying $(\star)$ to the pair $(B, A)$ in place of $(A, B)$, using the very same construction of (II) and invariance of (III), neither of which depends on the labelling of the two source/target sets. Hence
\[
    A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C,
\]
as required.
\end{proof}

\end{document}

Lemma — $\sigma$-separation is symmetric

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg, the walk definition of def def:walks, item i., the $\sigma$-blocking predicate (“$\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked”) of def def:sigma_blocking, and the $i\sigma$-/$\sigma$-separation predicates of def def:sigma_separation. Suppose \[ J = \emptyset, \] so that, by def def:sigma_separation, item 4, the $\sigma$-separation abbreviation \[ A \sPerp_G B \given C \;\; := \;\; A \isPerp_G B \given C \] is in scope (and, as recorded there, $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ unfolds under $J = \emptyset$ to: for every integer $n \ge 0$ and every walk $\pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ in $G$ in the sense of def def:walks, item i., with $v_0 \in A$ and $v_n \in B$, the walk $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked in the sense of def def:sigma_blocking). Let $A, B, C \ins V$ be subsets of nodes — equivalently $A, B, C \ins J \cup V$, since $J = \emptyset$; $A$, $B$, $C$ are not assumed to be pairwise disjoint, nor non-empty.

Then $\sigma$-separation in $G$ is symmetric in its first two arguments: \[ A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C. \]

Proof.

The proof has three main beats:

  1. (I) unfold $\sPerp_G$ via def def:sigma_separation, item 4, into the universal-over-walks statement of def def:sigma_separation, item 1, and reduce the biconditional to its forward implication (the converse follows by swapping $A$ and $B$ in the same argument);
  2. (II) construct an explicit walk-reversal involution $\pi \mapsto \pi^*$ that is a bijection between walks $v_0 \to v_n$ and walks $v_n \to v_0$ in $G$ in the sense of def def:walks, item i.; and
  3. (III) show that the $C$-$\sigma$-blocking predicate of def def:sigma_blocking is invariant under that reversal, so that the universal-over-walks statement is symmetric in $A$ and $B$.

The substantive content lives in beats (II) and (III): once walk reversal is set up as an involution that maps $C$-$\sigma$-blocked walks to $C$-$\sigma$-blocked walks (and back), the biconditional drops out by direct chase.

(I) Unfold and reduce to one direction. Throughout, fix $G = (J, V, E, L)$ a CDMG (def def-cdmg) with $J = \emptyset$, and $A, B, C \ins V = J \cup V$. By def def:sigma_separation, item 4, the abbreviation $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ is in scope and unfolds (under $J = \emptyset$, so $J \cup B = B$ and $J \cup A = A$) to \[ A \sPerp_G B \given C \quad\iff\quad \forall\, n \ge 0, \;\forall\, \text{walks } \pi = (v_0, a_0, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n) \text{ in } G \text{ with } v_0 \in A,\, v_n \in B :\; \pi \text{ is } C\text{-}\sigma\text{-blocked}, \] where “walk in $G$” is in the sense of def def:walks, item i., and “$C$-$\sigma$-blocked” is in the sense of def def:sigma_blocking. Symmetrically $B \sPerp_G A \given C$ unfolds to the analogous universal over walks $\pi'$ in $G$ with $v_0' \in B$ and $v_n' \in A$.

The biconditional we have to prove is symmetric in the roles of $A$ and $B$: the right-hand side $B \sPerp_G A \given C$ is obtained from the left-hand side $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ by literally swapping the symbols $A$ and $B$. It therefore suffices to prove the forward implication \[ A \sPerp_G B \given C \;\implies\; B \sPerp_G A \given C; \tag{$\star$} \] the converse implication then follows by applying $(\star)$ to the pair $(B, A)$ in place of $(A, B)$. From now on we assume $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ and aim to show $B \sPerp_G A \given C$.

(II) Walk reversal. We construct, for every $n \ge 0$ and every walk $\pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ in $G$ (def def:walks, item i.), a reversed walk $\pi^*$ from $v_n$ to $v_0$ in $G$. The construction is per slot, by the walk constraint of def def:walks, item i.

For $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$, the walk constraint at slot $i$ of $\pi$ states \[ \bigl(a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \;\land\; a_i \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \;\land\; a_i \in E\bigr). \] The slot $i$ of $\pi$ contributes an edge $a_i \in E \cup L$ to the walk; we define the reversed edge $a_i^*$ at slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ as follows, by case-analysis on which disjunct of the walk constraint fires at slot $i$ of $\pi$:

  • [(F$_E$)] If $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (directed edge of $G$ with tail $v_i$ and head $v_{i+1}$, traversed forwards from $v_i$ to $v_{i+1}$ on $\pi$), set \[ a_i^* := (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E, \] i.e.\ the same directed graph-edge, but now occupying slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ between the nodes $v_{i+1}$ (left) and $v_i$ (right). The walk constraint of def def:walks, item i.\ at slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ is then satisfied via its second disjunct ($a_i^* = (\text{right-node}, \text{left-node}) = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$); a directed graph-edge that was traversed forwards on $\pi$ is read backwards on $\pi^*$.
  • [(B$_E$)] If $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$ (directed graph-edge with tail $v_{i+1}$ and head $v_i$, traversed backwards from $v_i$ to $v_{i+1}$ on $\pi$), set \[ a_i^* := (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E, \] i.e.\ the same directed graph-edge, now occupying slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ between $v_{i+1}$ (left) and $v_i$ (right). The walk constraint at slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ is satisfied via its first disjunct ($a_i^* = (\text{left-node}, \text{right-node}) = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E \subseteq E \cup L$); a directed graph-edge traversed backwards on $\pi$ becomes one traversed forwards on $\pi^*$.
  • [(L)] If $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$ (bidirected graph-edge of $G$), set \[ a_i^* := (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in L. \] Here we use the standing convention of def def-cdmg that the bidirected-edge set $L$ consists of unordered (symmetric) pairs of nodes: if $(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$ then $(v_{i+1}, v_i) \in L$. (Equivalently, $L$ is closed under the swap $(u, w) \leftrightarrow (w, u)$; in the LN-typed graph-edge set, an $L$-edge is unoriented.) The walk constraint at slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ is satisfied via its first disjunct ($a_i^* = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in L \subseteq E \cup L$).

The reversed walk is then the tuple \[ \pi^* \;:=\; (v_n, \; a_{n-1}^*, \; v_{n-1}, \; a_{n-2}^*, \; \dots, \; a_1^*, \; v_1, \; a_0^*, \; v_0), \] i.e.\ the same node sequence read in reverse, paired with the slot-by-slot edge construction above. By the per-slot verification, $\pi^*$ satisfies the walk constraint of def def:walks, item i.\ at each of its $n$ slots, so $\pi^*$ is a walk in $G$ from $v_n$ to $v_0$.

Involution. Each of the three cases above is its own inverse: applying the construction once produces an edge whose “forward” vs.\ “backward” traversal is flipped, and applying it a second time flips it back. Concretely, in case (F$_E$) the reverse of $\pi^*$ at slot $n - 1 - (n - 1 - i) = i$ is again $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (we read $\pi^*$'s slot $n - 1 - i$, whose stored edge is $(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$, in case (B$_E$) of the reversal construction applied to $\pi^*$ – the second-disjunct walk-constraint case – and recover the first-disjunct configuration of the original $\pi$). Cases (B$_E$) and (L) are analogous (the L case using $((v_{i+1}, v_i)$ swapped $) = (v_i, v_{i+1})$). Hence $(\pi^*)^* = \pi$ on the nose, so $\pi \mapsto \pi^*$ is an involution on the set of walks in $G$.

Bijection between walk-spaces. For fixed source-set $A$ and target-set $B$, the involution restricts to a bijection between \[ \mathcal{W}_{A \to B} \;:=\; \{\,\pi \text{ walk in } G : v_0 \in A,\, v_n \in B\,\} \quad\text{and}\quad \mathcal{W}_{B \to A} \;:=\; \{\,\pi^* \text{ walk in } G : v_0^* \in B,\, v_n^* \in A\,\}, \] since $\pi \in \mathcal{W}_{A \to B}$ has $v_0 \in A,\, v_n \in B$ and $\pi^*$ has its start-node equal to $\pi$'s end-node $v_n \in B$ and its end-node equal to $\pi$'s start-node $v_0 \in A$, so $\pi^* \in \mathcal{W}_{B \to A}$, and conversely by involution. (This resolves the wording-check item walks_same_regardless_of_direction_ambiguous recorded in the workspace scratchpad for this row: walks are ordered sequences in the sense of def def:walks, item i., and the bijection between the two direction-sets is the explicit involution $\pi \leftrightarrow \pi^*$, not literal set equality.)

(III) $\sigma$-blocking is invariant under reversal. We claim that, for every walk $\pi$ in $G$, \[ \pi \text{ is } C\text{-}\sigma\text{-blocked} \quad\iff\quad \pi^* \text{ is } C\text{-}\sigma\text{-blocked}, \tag{$\dagger$} \] both in the sense of def def:sigma_blocking. By the involution $(\pi^*)^* = \pi$, the two directions of $(\dagger)$ are mutually each other's converses, so it suffices to prove the forward direction. We do so by tracing each of the two existential disjuncts of def def:sigma_blocking, separately, through the position-shift $k \leftrightarrow n - k$ induced by reversal.

Position-shift bookkeeping. By construction (II), the node at position $k$ on $\pi$ – namely $v_k$ – is the node at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ (which reads $\pi$'s node sequence backwards). The walk-incident edges at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ are slot $n - k - 1$ of $\pi^*$ (when $n - k \ge 1$) and slot $n - k$ of $\pi^*$ (when $n - k \le n - 1$). By the slot indexing of (II), slot $j$ of $\pi^*$ corresponds to slot $n - 1 - j$ of $\pi$; so:

  • slot $n - k - 1$ of $\pi^*$ corresponds to slot $n - 1 - (n - k - 1) = k$ of $\pi$ (i.e.\ to $a_k$, the right-incident slot at position $k$ on $\pi$), with the edge stored as $a_k^*$ per case (F$_E$)/(B$_E$)/(L) above;
  • slot $n - k$ of $\pi^*$ corresponds to slot $n - 1 - (n - k) = k - 1$ of $\pi$ (i.e.\ to $a_{k-1}$, the left-incident slot at position $k$ on $\pi$), with the edge stored as $a_{k-1}^*$.

In words: the left-incident slot of $\pi^*$ at position $n - k$ is the reversal of the right-incident slot of $\pi$ at position $k$, and the right-incident slot of $\pi^*$ at position $n - k$ is the reversal of the left-incident slot of $\pi$ at position $k$. The walk-incident index sets at position $k$ on $\pi$ and at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ are therefore in canonical bijection (with the two slots swapped).

(III.a) Collider invariance. We show: position $k$ is a collider on $\pi$ (def def:collider_noncollider, item ii.) iff position $n - k$ is a collider on $\pi^*$.

Per def def:collider_noncollider, item ii., position $k$ is a collider on $\pi$ iff $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = 2$, i.e.\ both walk-incident edges at position $k$ – $a_{k-1}$ (slot $k - 1$, present when $k \ge 1$) and $a_k$ (slot $k$, present when $k \le n - 1$) – are “edges into $v_k$” in the sense of def def:collider_noncollider (the formal predicate from def def-edge-relations, item ii.: “$a_i \in E$ with right-component $v_k$, or $a_i \in L$ with either component $v_k$”).

We must show that, under the bijection of walk-incident slots described above, “edge into $v_k$ at slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$” translates to “edge into $v_k$ at slot $n - k$ of $\pi^*$” (the corresponding reversed-slot), and similarly slot $k$ of $\pi$ translates to slot $n - k - 1$ of $\pi^*$. We check each of the three case-clauses (F$_E$), (B$_E$), (L) of the reversal construction:

  • Case (F$_E$) at slot $i$ of $\pi$: $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$. For $\pi$: $a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ iff its $E$-clause fires with second component $v_k$, iff $v_{i+1} = v_k$. (This happens at $i = k - 1$, giving an edge into $v_k$ at slot $k - 1$.) For $\pi^*$ at slot $n - 1 - i$: $a_i^* = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$, still a directed graph-edge, occupying slot $n - 1 - i$ of $\pi^*$ between the nodes $v_{i+1}$ (left) and $v_i$ (right). The right-component of $a_i^*$, in the sense of the $E$-clause of def def-edge-relations, item ii., is $v_{i+1}$ (the second component of the stored ordered pair). So $a_i^*$ is an edge into $v_k$ at $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ iff $v_{i+1} = v_k$, exactly as for $\pi$. Both walks register the same arrowhead at $v_k$.
  • Case (B$_E$) at slot $i$ of $\pi$: $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$. For $\pi$: $a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ iff $v_i = v_k$. (This happens at $i = k$, giving an edge into $v_k$ at slot $k$.) For $\pi^*$ at slot $n - 1 - i$: $a_i^* = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$, with right-component $v_i$. So $a_i^*$ is an edge into $v_k$ at $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ iff $v_i = v_k$, exactly as for $\pi$.
  • Case (L) at slot $i$ of $\pi$: $a_i \in L$. For $\pi$: $a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ iff its $L$-clause fires, i.e.\ $v_k$ is one of $a_i$'s two endpoints in $L$. By symmetry of $L$, this depends only on the underlying bidirected graph-edge – a property invariant under the swap performed by case (L). For $\pi^*$: $a_i^* = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in L$ (same bidirected graph-edge as $a_i$, with the ordered-pair writing swapped). The $L$-clause then likewise fires iff $v_k$ is an endpoint – the same condition.

In each case, “$a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ in $G$ along $\pi$” is equivalent to “$a_i^*$ is an edge into $v_k$ in $G$ along $\pi^*$ at the corresponding reversed slot”.

Combining over the two walk-incident slots at position $k$ on $\pi$ (and at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$), we get \[ \mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \;=\; \mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k). \] In particular $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = 2$ iff $\mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k) = 2$, so position $k$ is a collider on $\pi$ iff position $n - k$ is a collider on $\pi^*$ (def def:collider_noncollider, item ii.). The same equation $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = \mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k)$ gives the corresponding non-collider statement $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1 \iff \mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k) \le 1$ (def def:collider_noncollider, item i.), which we use below.

(III.b) Blockable non-collider invariance. We show: position $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ (def def:unblockable_noncollider, paragraph “Blockable non-collider on $\pi$”) iff position $n - k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi^*$.

Per def def:unblockable_noncollider, position $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ iff (i) it is a non-collider on $\pi$ (def def:collider_noncollider, item i.), AND (ii) the blockable disjunction holds at $k$ on $\pi$: \[ k \in \{0, n\} \;\;\lor\;\; \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k-1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr) \;\;\lor\;\; \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k+1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr). \] We show invariance of clauses (i) and (ii) separately.

  • Clause (i) – non-collider status – is invariant under the position-shift $k \leftrightarrow n - k$ by (III.a): $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1 \iff \mathrm{ah}_{\pi^*}(n - k) \le 1$.
  • Clause (ii) – the blockable disjunction – we check each of the three disjuncts. \begin{itemize}
  • End-position $k \in \{0, n\}$. Under the position-shift $k \mapsto n - k$, the set $\{0, n\}$ is mapped to itself (with $0 \leftrightarrow n$), so $k \in \{0, n\}$ on $\pi$ iff $n - k \in \{0, n\}$ on $\pi^*$.
  • Outgoing E-walk-edge at slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$ ($a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, with $v_{k-1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$). This is configuration (B$_E$) of slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$ (with $v_{i+1} := v_k$ and $v_i := v_{k-1}$ in case (B$_E$)'s notation, the directed graph-edge being $(v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, tail $v_k$, head $v_{k-1}$, traversed backwards on $\pi$). Under reversal, case (B$_E$) sends slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$ to slot $n - 1 - (k - 1) = n - k$ of $\pi^*$, with stored edge $a_{k-1}^* = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$. The first disjunct of the walk constraint at slot $n - k$ of $\pi^*$ then fires: $a_{k-1}^* = (v_k, v_{k-1}) = (\text{left-node at slot } n - k \text{ of } \pi^*, \text{right-node at slot } n - k \text{ of } \pi^*)$ – because the left node of slot $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ is the position-$n - k$ node $v_k$ and the right node is the position-$(n - k + 1)$ node $v_{k-1}$ – and lives in $E$. Reading this against the predicate “outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ at $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ on the right-incident slot $n - k$” from def def:unblockable_noncollider: the predicate “$a_i \in E$ with first component $v_k$” fires for $a_{k-1}^*$ (its first component is $v_k$, by construction), so $a_{k-1}^*$ is the outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ on the right-incident slot of $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$, with the other endpoint along $\pi^*$ being $v_{k-1}$ – the same node as the other endpoint along $\pi$.

    Conversely, an outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ on the right-incident slot of $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ – i.e.\ $a_{k-1}^* = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ – reverses (by involution) to the same directed graph-edge at slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$. Specifically, applying construction (II) to $\pi^*$ at slot $n - k$ via case (F$_E$) gives back $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ at slot $k - 1$ of $\pi$, with tail $v_k$ at the position-$k$ end – precisely the original outgoing E-walk-edge on $\pi$. Hence the “outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on the left-incident slot of $\pi$” corresponds bijectively to the “outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $n - k$ on the right-incident slot of $\pi^*$”.

  • Outgoing E-walk-edge at slot $k$ of $\pi$ ($a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$, with $v_{k+1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$). This is configuration (F$_E$) of slot $k$ of $\pi$ (directed graph-edge $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$, tail $v_k$, head $v_{k+1}$, traversed forwards on $\pi$). Under reversal, case (F$_E$) sends slot $k$ of $\pi$ to slot $n - 1 - k$ of $\pi^*$, with stored edge $a_k^* = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. At slot $n - 1 - k$ of $\pi^*$, the left node is $v_{k+1}$ and the right node is $v_k$; the second disjunct of the walk constraint fires: $a_k^* = (\text{right-node}, \text{left-node}) = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. Reading this against the predicate “outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ at $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$ on the left-incident slot $n - k - 1$” from def def:unblockable_noncollider: the predicate “$a_i \in E$ with first component $v_k$” fires for $a_k^*$ (its first component is $v_k$, by construction), so $a_k^*$ is the outgoing E-walk-edge of $v_k$ on the left-incident slot of $\pi^*$'s position $n - k$, with the other endpoint along $\pi^*$ being $v_{k+1}$ – the same node as the other endpoint along $\pi$. \end{itemize}

The disjunction at $k$ on $\pi$ – end-position OR left-incident-slot outgoing E-walk-edge to a non-$\Sc^G(v_k)$ node OR right-incident-slot outgoing E-walk-edge to a non-$\Sc^G(v_k)$ node – maps under the position-shift $k \leftrightarrow n - k$ to the disjunction at $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ – end-position OR right-incident-slot outgoing E-walk-edge OR left-incident-slot outgoing E-walk-edge – which is the same three-disjunct, just with the two “outgoing” disjuncts in swapped order. The “other endpoint $\notin \Sc^G(v_k)$” clause attaches to the underlying graph-edge's other endpoint and to the node $v_k$ at the position, both of which transport verbatim under reversal; the membership predicate $\Sc^G(\cdot) = \Anc^G(\cdot) \cap \Desc^G(\cdot)$ (def def_3_5, item vii.) is a property of $G$ alone – not of the walk – and is therefore unaffected by reversal.

Combining: clause (i) and clause (ii) at $k$ on $\pi$ jointly transport, by (III.a) and the case-by-case check above, to clause (i) and clause (ii) at $n - k$ on $\pi^*$. Hence position $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ iff position $n - k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi^*$.

(III.c) $C$-$\sigma$-blocking invariance. We now show $(\dagger)$. Per def def:sigma_blocking, $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked iff at least one of the following two existentials holds:

  • [(B1)] there exists a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ such that $k$ is a collider on $\pi$ and $v_k \notin \Anc^G(C)$;
  • [(B2)] there exists a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ such that $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ and $v_k \in C$.

We must show the same disjunction holds for $\pi^*$.

Suppose (B1) holds for $\pi$, with witness position $k$ and node $v_k$. By (III.a), position $n - k$ is a collider on $\pi^*$, and the node at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ is the same node $v_k$ (the node identity transports under reversal: $\pi^*$'s node-sequence is just $\pi$'s node-sequence read backwards). The condition $v_k \notin \Anc^G(C)$ is a condition on the node $v_k$ and on $G$ alone (def def_3_5, item iv.; the ancestor set in $G$ is independent of any walk), so it is unaffected by which walk we are considering. Hence position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ is a collider with $v_k \notin \Anc^G(C)$, so (B1) holds for $\pi^*$ at position $n - k$.

Suppose (B2) holds for $\pi$, with witness position $k$ and node $v_k$. By (III.b), position $n - k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi^*$, and the node at position $n - k$ on $\pi^*$ is $v_k$. The condition $v_k \in C$ is a condition on the node $v_k$ alone, so it transports verbatim. Hence (B2) holds for $\pi^*$ at position $n - k$.

In either case, $\pi^*$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked. So $\pi$ $C$-$\sigma$-blocked $\Rightarrow$ $\pi^*$ $C$-$\sigma$-blocked; by the involution argument noted above, the converse follows, and $(\dagger)$ is established.

(IV) The forward implication $(\star)$. Assume $A \sPerp_G B \given C$. By (I), this says: for every walk $\pi$ in $G$ with $v_0 \in A$ and $v_n \in B$, $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked.

Let $\pi' = (v_0', a_0', \dots, a_{n-1}', v_n')$ be an arbitrary walk in $G$ with $v_0' \in B$ and $v_n' \in A$. We must show $\pi'$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked. Apply the reversal construction of (II) to $\pi'$: by the bijection $\mathcal{W}_{B \to A} \to \mathcal{W}_{A \to B}$ (specialised at source-set $A$ and target-set $B$), $(\pi')^*$ is a walk in $G$ with start-node $v_n' \in A$ and end-node $v_0' \in B$, so $(\pi')^* \in \mathcal{W}_{A \to B}$. By the hypothesis $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ applied to $(\pi')^*$, the walk $(\pi')^*$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked. By (III) applied to $\pi'$ – specifically, $(\dagger)$ applied to $\pi'$ gives $\pi'$ $C$-$\sigma$-blocked iff $(\pi')^*$ $C$-$\sigma$-blocked – the walk $\pi'$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked. Since $\pi'$ was arbitrary, $B \sPerp_G A \given C$ holds, by the unfolding in (I).

(V) Conclusion. By the reduction in (I), the biconditional $A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C$ follows from the forward implication $(\star)$, which is (IV); the converse implication is obtained by applying $(\star)$ to the pair $(B, A)$ in place of $(A, B)$, using the very same construction of (II) and invariance of (III), neither of which depends on the labelling of the two source/target sets. Hence \[ A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C, \] as required.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `sigma_separation_symmetric` and its walk-reversal helpers. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for the walk-reversal pipeline in particular: the `Sym2 Node` quotient used by `.bidir` steps inherits its `DecidableEq` instance from `Node`, so membership of an L-edge in `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` stays decidable across `WalkStep.reverse`, and the σ-blocking predicate's per-position case analysis reduces to computable `Finset` membership on `G.J ∪ G.V`. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability propagates to `Sym2 Node` so `Finset (Sym2 Node)` membership stays computable across walk reversal.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
theorem sigma_separation_symmetric
    (G : CDMG Node) (hJ : G.J = ∅) (A B C : Set Node)
    (hA : A ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hB : B ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V)
    (hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) :
    G.IsSigmaSeparated hJ A B C hA hB hC ↔
      G.IsSigmaSeparated hJ B A C hB hA hC
lean_blocks.explanation: This block is the statement of the row's headline claim: $\sigma$-separation in a CDMG is symmetric in its first two set arguments. The theorem takes a graph `G : CDMG Node`, a propositional hypothesis `hJ : G.J = ∅` carving out the regime in which the $\sigma$-name is in scope (per `def_3_18` item 4), three vertex subsets `A B C : Set Node`, and three subset-typing proofs `hA hB hC` against `↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V`. The `↑` coercions land the `Finset Node`-valued projections `G.J` and `G.V` into `Set Node` so `∪` is `Set` union. The conclusion is a biconditional on `IsSigmaSeparated` — the $J=\emptyset$-named predicate, not the underlying `IsISigmaSeparated` — with the unique well-typed swap on the right: `A`, `B` exchange and their typing evidence `hA`, `hB` swaps alongside, while `C, hC` stay put (the conditioning set is symmetric in the LN and `hC` types against the unchanged `C`). The argument order mirrors `IsSigmaSeparated`'s positional binding byte-for-byte, so `rw [sigma_separation_symmetric hJ A B C hA hB hC]` transports the predicate across the swap without re-deriving typing premises.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ref: claim_3_22 (main) — σ-separation is symmetric in its first two set arguments under `G.J = ∅`.
theorem sigma_separation_symmetric
    -- `G` is the ambient CDMG; `hJ` is the LN's `J = ∅` scope condition that activates the σ-name.
    (G : CDMG Node) (hJ : G.J = ∅) (A B C : Set Node)
    -- Typing premises: each set lives in the graph's node carrier `↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V` (coercions land `Finset`-valued projections into `Set`).
    (hA : A ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hB : B ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V)
    (hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) :
    -- Biconditional `↔`, mirroring the LN's `\iff`; predicate name `IsSigmaSeparated` (the σ-named alias under `hJ`).
    G.IsSigmaSeparated hJ A B C hA hB hC ↔
      -- RHS swap: `A ↔ B` and `hA ↔ hB` swap alongside (positional typing evidence); `C, hC` stay put.
      G.IsSigmaSeparated hJ B A C hB hA hC

design_choices:
**The σ-name on both sides of the biconditional.** The theorem uses `IsSigmaSeparated` rather than the underlying `IsISigmaSeparated` predicate it abbreviates under `hJ : G.J = ∅`. The lecture notes state the claim purely in σ-language ($A \sPerp_G B \given C$), so using the σ-named predicate at the signature level preserves a one-to-one notes-to-Lean correspondence. A parallel iσ-version can be stated separately downstream if ever needed; it is not part of this claim.

**`hJ : G.J = ∅` as an explicit propositional premise.** The σ-separation notation is *defined* only under an empty input-node set, so `hJ` is a notational scope condition, not an extra hypothesis bolted onto a more general claim. We considered defining a `J = ∅`-restricted CDMG subtype and packaging `hJ` into the structure, but that would force every downstream user to repack their CDMG before invoking symmetry. Baking `hJ` into a side definition was likewise rejected, as it would diverge from the upstream predicate's positional binding. Taking `hJ` as a positional theorem hypothesis matches the notes' "let $G$ be a CDMG; suppose $J = \emptyset$" reading and lets downstream consumers discharge `hJ` from their own ambient hypotheses.

**Three subset typing premises passed through verbatim.** Under `hJ`, the typing `A ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V` is equivalent to `A ⊆ ↑G.V`. We deliberately do not reformulate to the simpler form: pulling the typing premises through unmodified keeps the Lean signature faithful to the upstream `IsSigmaSeparated` contract, and downstream consumers can run the equivalence locally when they have the simpler hypothesis on hand.

**Biconditional rather than two one-direction implications.** The notes state `\iff`, so the Lean conclusion uses `↔` — a single rewrite-friendly shape downstream consumers will reach for to transport σ-separation across the swap. A `Symmetric`-typeclass / 2-ary-relation encoding was rejected because `IsSigmaSeparated` is 3-ary in `(A, B, C)`; folding it into `Symmetric` would force consumers to curry away `C, hC` at every use site, obscuring the LN correspondence.

**A custom local walk-reversal pipeline, not a generic walk-reversal library.** The proof builds `WalkStep.reverse`, `Walk.reverse`, and per-predicate invariance lemmas (`isCollider_reverse`, `isBlockableNonCollider_reverse`, `isSigmaBlockedGiven_reverse`) specifically for this file. A generic reversal layer in the underlying `Walks` module was considered but rejected: under $J = \emptyset$ the symmetry's substance is per-position blockability, which depends only on incident-edge geometry at each vertex (not on traversal direction), and the targeted helpers stay close to that geometric content while keeping the dependency chain inside this row.
